patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 17-year-old anxious but clear-speaking high school student who also works part-time as a grocery cashier. Start with: you get tired and weak very fast, like your muscles “give up,” and it’s messing up school, work, and stairs/getting up from a chair. Volunteer that your eyelids feel heavy by day’s end, resting helps briefly, and you have a noticeable spine curve with one shoulder higher.

If asked about specific symptoms, describe slurred/mushy speech when tired, trouble chewing tougher foods, occasional coughing/choking on drinks, difficulty holding arms overhead (washing hair), worse with cold/illness, and sometimes shortness of breath when worn out. Mention you’ve had diabetes since infancy and use insulin.

Do not bring up unless directly asked: being “floppy” since childhood, delayed milestones, morning headaches/unrefreshed sleep, frequent bronchitis.

Resist discussing drooling, avoiding eating in public, and needing to sleep propped up; minimize unless the clinician is very supportive.

Express misconceptions: worry it’s low blood sugar, anxiety, being out of shape, or MS.

When shown empathy, soften, admit you’re scared, and ask about cost/insurance; assume genetics/PT/bracing are unaffordable and fear job loss if you disclose anything.

Introduce misconceptions: worry about hereditary swelling (lip/eyelid episodes after eating out), fat-digestion problem after fatty meals, parathyroid/calcium issue due to past kidney stones, or rare genetic disease like your cousin.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis explicitly as mercury poisoning and links symptoms/exam findings to the exposure history.
Explains chelation in patient-friendly terms, including purpose (bind/aid elimination of mercury), expected benefits, and major risks/monitoring needs.
Advises immediate cessation/avoidance of the suspected mercury source and provides practical exposure-reduction guidance (e.g., workplace/household handling, avoiding high-mercury fish if relevant).
Performs an allergy check before prescribing any chelator; if allergies are unknown, asks and documents uncertainty and proceeds cautiously.

Performs a contraindication check before prescribing chelation (e.g., pregnancy status when relevant, renal/hepatic impairment, G6PD deficiency considerations depending on chelator, inability to tolerate oral meds, severe vomiting, altered mental status).
Performs a drug interaction check before prescribing (non-critical but expected), especially for concurrent nephrotoxic agents or medications that may complicate renal/hepatic monitoring.

Chooses chelation consistent with clinical context and severity (e.g., succimer for stable symptomatic inorganic/elemental exposure; dimercaprol ± EDTA for severe inorganic mercury poisoning; avoids inappropriate chelation for low-level exposure without symptoms).

Recognizes that elemental mercury vapor inhalation vs inorganic salt ingestion vs organic (methylmercury) exposure have different clinical patterns and management considerations, and clarifies likely type/source.

Recommends appropriate confirmatory testing and monitoring (e.g., blood/urine mercury with correct specimen selection, CBC/CMP/renal function, urinalysis; neuro exam; ECG if indicated) without delaying urgent stabilization/referral when severe.

Assesses and addresses red flags requiring emergency care/referral (e.g., respiratory distress after vapor inhalation, severe GI symptoms/dehydration, altered mental status, seizures, significant kidney injury, hemodynamic instability).
Provides a clear follow-up/monitoring plan in narrative even if not using a follow-up tool (e.g., recheck labs, symptom monitoring, adherence, adverse effects, and exposure remediation).

Does not provide unsafe advice such as attempting home cleanup of mercury spills without proper guidance, using unproven detox regimens, or recommending chelation without appropriate evaluation/monitoring.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 29-year-old male forklift operator. Be vague and a bit evasive; give short answers unless asked directly. Start by saying: “My ankles keep giving out and I keep tripping over my own feet.” Volunteer only: feet feel weak like you can’t lift them well, long-standing high arches with shoes wearing on the outside, skinny calves, and that it’s been slowly worsening for years.

If asked specifics, add: toes drag on rugs/curbs, frequent ankle sprains even on flat ground, numb/asleep toes, don’t notice small cuts, hands tire with buttons/jars and weaker grip, cold worsens numbness/clumsiness, and a relative had “weak ankles”/braces.

Do NOT mention unless pressed: high-stepping to avoid toe catch, avoiding stairs/uneven ground, toe curling/can’t spread toes, worse in the dark, needing extra shoe support.

If empathy offered, soften briefly (“Yeah… it’s embarrassing”) but then deflect. If asked what you think, suggest pinched back nerve, being out of shape, diabetes, or poor circulation. Express worry about costs, insurance, time off work; dismiss PT/orthotics as unaffordable unless reassured.

If asked about other symptoms, report intermittent low-grade fevers/chills for a week, new shortness of breath on one flight of stairs, headaches/lightheadedness on standing, heavier last period, bone/joint aches (low back/hips), low appetite and a few pounds of unintentional weight loss.
